dialogue.utterance: Dispatch to Unit 3 responding for chest pain in Richmond.
Hi, my name is Alex. I'm an EMT with the rescue squad. What made you call 911 today?
Uh, chest pain and shortness of breath started this morning, and I've also had some left leg pain since yesterday.
Are you alert and oriented to your name, the date, and what's happening around you?
Yeah, I'm aware of where I am and can see fine.
Can you tell me your full name, please?
My name is James. What day is it today?
It's March 3rd. Can you tell me what happened to cause your symptoms?
Your airway is clear and patent. Can you take a few deep breaths for me?
Your breathing is very fast, around 50 breaths per minute, and you're tachypneic but not using accessory muscles.
Pulse is 97 and regular. Skin is warm, dry with normal color. No bleeding is noted.
What symptoms are you experiencing besides the chest and leg pain?
I have allergies to Naprosyn and Tramadol.
Are you taking any medications now or in the past?
I've had clotting issues, DVTs, and PEs before. I take Eliquis, but I can't now due to insurance issues.
Can you tell me the last time you ate or drank something?

dialogue.micro_intent: S.A.M.P.L.E. events leading up to the event
dialogue.utterance: The leg pain started yesterday. This morning I noticed chest pain and shortness of breath.
When did the chest pain begin?
It began this morning. It's worse on deep inspiration.
Can you describe the quality of your chest and leg pain?
It's sharp and constant in the chest. The leg is really achy with tenderness all over the thigh.
On a scale of 0-10, how would you rate the pain?
Chest pain is about 8/10. Leg pain is the worst-10/10.
Is there any swelling or redness on your legs?
The left leg is warm, swollen, and really tender to touch.
Your eyes are equal and reactive. I am going to shine a light in your eyes just to check; please look at me.
Okay, go ahead.
There is no jugular vein distention. Your neck appears normal.
Your breath sounds are clear and equal on both sides.
Does the breathing cause pain when I move?
Movement seems to aggravate your left leg pain. Would you rate it as 10/10 with movement?
Yes, just trying to shift causes the pain.
Pulse is at 97 beats per minute.
Respirations are 50 per minute.
Blood pressure is 124 over 85.
Oxygen saturation is 100% on room air.
Glucose cannot be obtained at this time.
EKG shows sinus rhythm with a borderline finding for age and sex.
We're placing you on 4-lead ECG monitoring to track your heart rhythm.
We're also doing a 12-lead ECG for more detailed cardiac assessment.
We're starting an IV in your left arm. This could be used for fluid administration if needed.
Your ETCO2 is 30mmHg, consistent with your rapid breathing.
IV access is gained in the left AC and is secure.
You're alert and oriented to person, place, time, and event.
Based on symptoms and vitals, we are managing you under chest pain-cardiac suspected protocol.
Your heart rate has decreased to 92.
Still tachypneic-breathing at 50 per minute.
Blood pressure is now 127 over 72.
Oxygen saturation remains at 99%.
IV is patent and functioning well.
With your history, do you think prior clots put you at risk for another?
I have 30+ past DVTs and around 10 PEs. I had to stop Eliquis after prison release.
Where would you prefer to go for treatment?
VCU Hospital is where I'd like to go, please.
I'll help you get on the stretcher. We will use a semi-Fowler's position for comfort and safety.
We're placing you in a semi-Fowler's position and securing you for transport.
That's good, my chest feels a little better.
Secured! Transport team is on standby.
Patient James May, 39 years old, is en route to VCU for chest pain and probable PE with 10/10 leg pain.
IV secured and EKG monitoring continued en route.
Your GCS is 15, meaning you're fully alert and oriented.
Airway remains clear, and you're using your normal posture to breathe.
Breathing rate is still 50, regular, and without signs of being labored.
Breath sounds remain clear bilaterally.
Your left leg is still hot and tender with mild swelling, but no deformity or angulation is noted.
It's okay; we don't always need the exact time. Just focusing on your condition now.
The final diagnosis will be up to the doctors, but based on your history, we're considering pulmonary embolism.
The glucose machine is not working, but you don't appear in distress for hypoglycemia.
We'll be careful with any movements to avoid worsening your leg pain.
I've had more DVTs and PEs than I can count, and I stopped my medication when I got out.
IV site is dry, patent, and showing no signs of infiltration.
127/72. Your systolic is slightly higher now than at the start.
No bruises, rashes, or noticeable skin irregularities.
Your skin is warm with a normal cap refill time of less than 2 seconds.
Skin color is normal.
No swelling, deformity, or other issues noted in the head or neck.

dialogue.role: nose, mouth and throat exam; Patient
dialogue.utterance: I tried to swallow but it's no problem.

dialogue.role: nose, mouth and throat exam; Partner
dialogue.utterance: No foreign bodies visible in the oral cavity or nose.
Still patent airway. No abnormal sounds or sensation of blockage.
No lymphadenopathy or masses in the neck.
I heard no wheezing or stridor. Sounds are present in all lobes bilaterally.
No masses, tenderness, or signs of acute abdomen assessed at this time.
Urine appearance and symptoms cannot be assessed at this time.
Right leg is normal with full PMS. Left leg has pain and swelling, but no deformity seen.
Radial pulses are equal, strong, and regular on both sides.
EKG remains in sinus rhythm. No acute ST changes present.
Continuous monitoring shows no impressive abnormalities related to acute cardiac compromise.
Skin turgor cannot be assessed at this time.
VCU hospital has been notified about our expected arrival time with the pre-arrival report.
You are doing well on the stretcher. Try to stay relaxed during transport.
ER is getting prepped based on concerns about your DVT/PE history.
If I end up back in the hospital, they can check my history and know the risks.
You're going to resuscitation. They'll take it from here.
Considering your DVT/PE history, quick transport is best for evaluation.
Let's get you to the car before we start monitoring for any changes en route.
Stretcher wheels are locked, IV secured; transport ready.
Flight team has been notified in case we need special assistance for your elevated weight.
Patient secure and ready for transport under cardiac protocol to VCU.
No nuchal rigidity, and your mental status is normal.
I don't hear any crackles, wheezes, or rales. Breathing is more rapid but not noisy.
I don't hear or feel any abnormal chest wall movement or asymmetry.
Hearing does not appear to be an issue; you understand everything we're saying.
No petechiae or purpura on your back or legs. Skin is intact otherwise.
Your hands and feet are warm, which is a good sign for peripheral circulation.
You mentioned having stopped Eliquis due to incarceration and lack of access post-release.
I'll set up continuous EKG monitoring as per protocol.
I'll check IV patency again in a few minutes before and during transport.
I've included all the medical history you gave us in the report for the hospital.
VCU ER was given your full set of vitals, EKG findings, and history for immediate triage.
Airway is still patent and no concerning sounds or signs.
Still at 99%, as desired on room air.
EKG unchanged as per latest reading-still in sinus rhythm.
Transport team will assist with moving you to the ambulance once we're ready.
No scars, bite marks, or any other lesions noted on the face or neck.
Fresh IV on the left AC is working and will be administered fluids if medically indicated.

dialogue.role: nose, mouth and throat exam; EMT
dialogue.utterance: Mouth is clear, oral mucosa normal with no signs of dehydration.

dialogue.role: nose, mouth and throat exam; EMT
dialogue.utterance: No signs of obstruction in the nose or mouth.
Teeth and gums look normal; no dental trauma or bleeding.
I'm checking your peripheral pulses now. Radial is strong.
No masses, tenderness, or signs of acute abdomen noted.
No history of issues with urination and no urinary signs are assessed at this time.
Back and flank show no abnormalities or sign of trauma.
Pulse has remained around 92 throughout the last few minutes.
IV is still in working condition, and we just rechecked that trace is correct.
You look good for transport. We'll keep monitoring you and report any changes.
Your chronic issues may complicate a new PE, so we want to be sure you're treated fast.
I'll go ahead and move you gently to the stretcher now.
Patient is now secured in semi-Fowler's position with EKG monitoring ongoing.
All interventions are set and synchronized with the monitoring.
Destination hospital is ready with a resuscitation team based on our report.
Resp still at 50/min with no increase in work of breathing.
